checks.scenario: A new agent orchestration framework drops on GitHub with 8,000 stars overnight. It claims to be '10x faster than LangChain' with a benchmark comparison on a synthetic task. You are currently using a simple custom agent loop that works well for your production use case.
checks.question: Applying the new tool evaluation checklist, what is the first question to ask?
options: A. Is it production-ready? Check the GitHub issues and open PRs
B. Does it solve a problem I actually have right now? If my current agent loop works, the answer is probably no.
C. Is the benchmark methodology valid? Compare the synthetic task to your real use case.
D. How many companies are using it in production? Wait for adoption before evaluating.
checks.explanation: The problem check is always first. A tool that does not solve your current problem should not receive evaluation time regardless of benchmark scores or star counts. The '10x faster' benchmark on a synthetic task does not answer 'does this help me?' If your current loop works for your use case, the bar for switching is high: measured evidence that it is better on your specific task, not a synthetic benchmark.

checks.scenario: You are 90 days out of the curriculum. You check your skills against the 5-pillar self-assessment and notice that your evaluation skills feel fluent (you ran evals at work twice this month), but your observability knowledge feels rusty. You cannot remember how to set up a Langfuse integration without looking it up.
checks.question: What is the correct response to this finding?
options: A. Re-read the Phase 07 lessons to refresh the knowledge
B. Add observability to your next project: instrument an existing service with Langfuse tracing this weekend. Building refreshes skills faster than reading.
C. Accept that observability is a specialized skill and you do not need it unless your role requires it
D. Look up the Langfuse documentation once, take notes, and move on
checks.explanation: The self-assessment is a diagnostic, not a reading trigger. When a skill feels rusty, the fix is building, not reading. Adding Langfuse tracing to an existing service in a weekend produces two things: working code you can reference, and a muscle memory refresh that reading documentation does not provide. Reading is useful for new knowledge. Building is how you maintain existing knowledge.

checks.scenario: A senior engineer on your team says: 'You should be reading these 3 AI newsletters, following 10 researchers on Twitter, and watching all the major AI conference talks to stay current.' You currently have about 3 hours per week for learning outside of work.
checks.question: What is the correct response to this advice?
options: A. Agree: staying current in AI requires broad information consumption

B. The advice optimizes for awareness over capability. With 3 hours per week, building one small project produces more lasting skill than reading 3 newsletters. Focus the reading on 3-4 high-signal sources and spend the majority of time building.
C. Follow the advice for 30 days and see if you feel more current
D. Replace all reading with building: stop following any news sources entirely
checks.explanation: Broad information consumption feels productive but does not build skills. With 3 hours per week, the tradeoff is real: every hour reading a newsletter is an hour not spent building. The 4 curated sources (Anthropic release notes, Latent Space, applied-llms.org, Hamel's blog) cover the high-signal inputs. The remaining time should go to building. The 20% exploration time pattern is: 20% of engineering time on exploration, not 80% on reading about exploration.

checks.scenario: Anthropic releases a new version of Claude with significantly improved function calling. The release notes show 40% improvement on the BFCL benchmark. You have a production agent that uses tool calling.
checks.question: Applying the foundation vs. surface area framework, what layer is this change on, and what should you do?
options: A. This is a foundation change: update your agent patterns and system prompts immediately
B. This is surface area change (model version): apply your existing golden set eval to the new model, measure whether it improves your specific use case, and upgrade if the eval confirms improvement
C. This is noise: wait 6 months for the community to validate the benchmark
D. This is surface area: ignore it unless the old model is deprecated
checks.explanation: A model version change is surface area, not foundation. Your agent patterns, golden set, and eval methodology (all foundations) remain unchanged. The correct action: run your golden set eval on the new model, compare to your baseline, and upgrade if your specific task improves. The BFCL benchmark improvement is a hint, not a guarantee - benchmarks are designed by their creators. Your golden set is designed for your use case.

checks.scenario: You are completing the 5-pillar self-assessment and reach Pillar 4 (AI Evaluation). You write: 'I can now build a golden set and run LLM-as-judge evals.' But when you try to fill in the evidence section, you realize your only example is from a curriculum exercise, not a production or real-world project.
checks.question: What does this indicate, and what is the next step?
options: A. This is fine: curriculum projects are real projects

B. The skill is theoretical, not operational. The next step is to apply it to a new problem you have not solved before: pick any real use case, build a golden set, and run the eval. Document the go/no-go decision.
C. Add a note that the curriculum exercise was high-quality and move on
D. The evidence requirement is too strict; any completed exercise counts
checks.explanation: The self-assessment asks for evidence because fluency requires having applied a skill in context, not just having completed it once under guided conditions. A curriculum exercise shows you can follow the pattern. Applying it to a new, self-directed problem shows you own the skill. The gap between 'completed the exercise' and 'applied it independently' is exactly what the self-assessment is designed to surface. The fix is a new project, not a re-read.

checks.scenario: You want to stay current on AI engineering but find yourself spending 2 hours per day reading AI Twitter, newsletters, and blog posts. You feel current on announcements but have not built anything new in 3 weeks.
checks.question: This pattern indicates which failure mode, and what is the fix?
options: A. This is fine: awareness of the field is essential for an AI engineer

B. This is the 'reading as a proxy for learning' trap. Cut information consumption to the 4 curated sources (15-30 min/day max) and allocate the recovered time to building. Awareness without building does not maintain skills.
C. The issue is the sources, not the volume: curate better sources
D. This pattern is only a problem if your job performance is suffering
checks.explanation: Reading AI news feels like staying current, but it does not build or maintain skills. The 20% time pattern allocates exploration time to building, not reading. 2 hours of daily reading with no building produces awareness of what others are building. 30 minutes of reading plus 1.5 hours of building produces both awareness and maintained capability. The 4 curated sources are chosen specifically because they are low-noise and high-signal; the remaining time is for building.

checks.scenario: In a job interview, the interviewer asks: 'How do you evaluate whether a new AI framework is worth adopting for your team?' You want to demonstrate that you have a systematic approach rather than chasing what is popular.
checks.question: What answer best demonstrates engineering judgment?
options: A. 'I watch what top companies are adopting and follow their lead'

B. 'I apply a 5-question filter: problem check, foundation check, evidence check, cost check, and replace check. The most important is whether there is production evidence it improves on what we already have - not a benchmark, but a case study from a team solving a similar problem.'
C. 'I evaluate the GitHub star count and community size as signals of production readiness'
D. 'I test every new framework on a small prototype before making a decision'
checks.explanation: The 5-question filter demonstrates systematic judgment rather than trend-following or ad-hoc evaluation. The specific emphasis on production evidence over benchmarks is the differentiating signal: it shows you understand that synthetic benchmarks do not predict performance on your specific use case. Testing on a prototype (option D) is part of the process, but without the upstream filter, you end up prototyping everything that looks interesting.

checks.scenario: You complete the curriculum and write your 'I can now...' statements for all 5 pillars. Pillar 3 (Agents) reads: 'I can now implement a multi-tool agent and measure whether it meets its quality target.' But the role you are targeting (FDE) emphasizes scoping and handoff, and Pillar 5 reads: 'I can now write an AI spec.' You feel significantly more confident in agents than in FDE skills.
checks.question: How should this self-assessment result shape your 90-day plan?
options: A. Prioritize deepening your strongest area (agents) since that is where you have the most leverage

B. The role you are targeting requires Pillar 5 more than Pillar 3. Allocate 60% of 90-day project time to FDE skills: build 3 projects that specifically practice scoping, demo delivery, and handoff. Use agents as the technical vehicle for those projects, not the focus.
C. Focus equally on all 5 pillars to maintain balance
D. Switch your target role to one that better matches your current strengths
checks.explanation: The self-assessment is most useful when it informs role-specific prioritization. Strong agents skills are a foundation for FDE work, but the differentiated skill the FDE role requires is scoping, delivery, and handoff - the Pillar 5 areas. The 90-day plan should use agents as the vehicle (you are comfortable there) while practicing FDE disciplines around the agent work: write the AI spec before building, deliver a demo to a simulated non-technical audience, write the handoff package. This strengthens the weak area without abandoning the strong one.
